data.title: AIPI: From Capped Upside To Rangebound Income Opportunity
data.description:
In May last year, I had issued a Hold rating for the REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF (AIPI). At that time, and for a few months following that rating, everything tagged AI was being lapped up by the market. That environment has changed. The AI trade has cooled, macro pressures and stagflation risks aside. Importantly, the core objective for income seekers is to be able to generate optimal income, whether through an option writing route or through capital appreciation of the underlying.

Last year AIPI was not posing much upside from the AI rally because of the capped nature of the covered call writing. Income was still supportive because some upside capture was still at play, although despite the option income, the aggressive yields meant NAV erosion possibilities, which is bad news for future payout size. You can see the effect of smaller payouts over time in the dividend payout chart below. This is despite any meaningful change in yield levels. That effect only amplifies if the underlying portfolio stagnates.

Theoretically, the strategy does not need a sharp rally in the underlying. What is actually and optimally needed is a supportive portfolio as a minimum, which then opens up option income in rangebound markets. The total returns can, in such a regime, even outperform the underlying portfolio itself. That regime is here today — after a reset in the AI trade, we could see exactly the perfect markets for AIPI. One where the risks of a further downside is more limited than it was in May last year, and the odds of a sharp rally also doesn't look imminent. A rangebound market is where option income works best. In short, compared to my old thesis, where the upside opportunity loss was far higher than the drawdown mitigation, we have now moved into a more symmetric market for option writing. That moves AIPI to Buy territory.

Performance Since May

The below chart shows the total return trajectory for AIPI, with AIQ and QQQ as anchors to help contextualize. Neither of the anchors have the same exact underlying portfolio as AIPI, but AIQ should show an approximate path for total return investors invested in the AI theme (AIPI has a much smaller high conviction portfolio of ~25 stocks compared to AIQ's broader ~90 stock exposure), and QQQ shows an even broader AI path without AIPI's high conviction positioning.

The total returns chart shows how AIPI expectedly was unable to capture the AI trade's upside in a strong bull market till October 2025, something even a broader AI portfolio like AIQ could. AIPI's total return trajectory (implies payouts reinvested and option income embedded on top of portfolio gains) traced a very broad exposure to AI like QQQ in the bull run. That often means bad news for holding through the cycle - if one of the sharpest AI rallies only means tracking a broader AI portfolio (like QQQ), then in even slightly cooling markets, the downside will be far higher. This is also in line with what we see, AIPI's upside is QQQ but the downside is probably as high as AIQ, and this despite the option income.

That also means the expected NAV erosion starts showing up aggressively in flat to negative regimes for the AI trade. While the NAV erosion threat was broadly contained till October, we have now seen 20-30% NAV erosion. The performance of AIPI since May overall upholds the concerns I had around the time of the earlier thesis. For a strategy that at best retains NAV in one of the best times for its underlying portfolio; NAV erosion should be expected to be the norm in most market conditions.

That is unlikely to be fixed ahead unless yields are moderated or reinvested. But three things matter in addition to NAV erosion — one, the quality of income, two, the degree of NAV erosion and three, the asymmetry of upside and downside moves. On these parameters, AIPI seems to have moved into a better regime today. And all those themes are inter-related too. A cooler regime for AI valuations imply we are no longer staring at capturing say 2% upside when underlying holdings are rallying by 10%, yet staring at all the risks of cooling ahead (like we were in May to October 2025), with minimal cushion from option income in drawdowns. That makes it a more symmetrical setup for AIPI now. A flat rangebound outlook also means slower upsides are captured, option premiums add to the total returns and overall, the quality of income is option dominated (rather than a return of capital which is structurally not allowed to grow well either due to the covered call upside capping setup). Since the underlying is flat, NAV erodes by the difference of payouts versus option income. This is an improvement from the most recent drawdown regime where NAV erodes due to corrections in the underlying as well.

Why The Rangebound View

A look at AIPI's portfolio explains my rangebound view on the portfolio. Around 70% of the portfolio is in enablers and adopters, with high beta and uncertainties at the moment. The enablers exposure is primarily in semis and AI infrastructure, where late cycle concerns are emerging. The trough in hyperscaler CapEx could indeed turn out to be higher than what markets fear at this point and AI could be the multi-decade secular tailwind it was positioned as, at the start of the cycle. Even if cyclicality returns in the semis space, we could see that later than the immediate next year too. But the odds of a multiple expansion definitely looks less possible at the moment. We do not have to move too far beyond the NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) stagnation over the past few months to verify this rangebound outlook in the semis space. I have written about how the tech heavyweights will likely remain rangebound unless earnings upgrades return, in a separate article on the Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG).

The next biggest exposure is in an even more uncertain bucket of adopters, dominated primarily by SaaS companies. This is where markets are gauging the effects of a steady state AI dynamics, commoditization risks and pricing pressures thereafter. While the bulk of corrections have already happened here, there is no urgency to rerate the pack (except a few pockets), unless certainty arrives. And that uncertainty will linger for a few quarters at least as the tech world evolves through AI's capabilities and positioning.

The hyperscaler pack is probably the most resilient group in AIPI's portfolio, but it is underweight compared to semis and SaaS. These companies are also going through their own CapEx drag pressures, but monetization uncertainties are not as binary as in the case of SaaS or cyclical as in the case of semis. The bigger question for this layer is the return on capex and the economics of the AI monetization phase. Some of the companies in this pack, like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) or Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) have corrected quite a bit too acknowledging the high spend regime. So I expect this layer to be relatively stable, but we are certainly not looking at immediate upsides, at least at a 2025-like pace.

How Well is AIPI Set Up?

AIPI writes calls on individual holdings rather than at an index level. I downloaded the full holdings as of April 2nd 2026 and saw covered calls written for almost the full holding notional value for the top 10 holdings. There are some mechanisms like a laddered call setup which means some upside capture is not ruled out (with some strikes far OTM), but this is an aggressive option writing setup as such.

That aggressive setup with a bit of upside room for underlying stocks is the perfect recipe for the current rangebound outlook, as we have discussed before. It ensures the portfolio generates flat to marginally positive returns over the next few quarters and the option layer generates decent income, especially if conditions remain as volatile as we have seen over the past month.

A Buy Call for the Next Few Quarters

A Buy for an income plan does not necessarily mean buying the underlying portfolio. So the AI tag does not help AIPI's thesis as much as it looks like. Income strategies should look at a moderately bullish thesis in the underlying, in the long term which AI comes with. That thesis is strengthened after massive corrections in the SaaS space and some cooling in semis and hyperscaler stocks. And beyond a point, such strategies do not need a sharp bull run versus a moderate one - beyond a threshold, the upside does not matter much (especially if it is within a short period of time). To that end, I was wary of the disproportionate downside risks in May last year, without commensurate benefits of the sharp upside getting captured. That thesis has now turned to a Buy. I expect the underlying portfolio to now be in an ideal position to keep the overall upside under check. Downside risks are not as high as earlier. And the option income should be able to add value than just cap upsides.
